clinical_coherence.notes: 75M African American with bilateral parkinsonism on metoclopramide for 3 years for gastroparesis. Third case with same metoclopramide confound. Presentation is well-detailed with bilateral cogwheel rigidity (L>R), bradykinesia, masked facies, micrographia. Wife uncertain about whether gait changes preceded medication.

tool_output_consistency.notes: MRI shows subtle ventricular enlargement mildly out of proportion to atrophy - adds NPH to differential, which is a nice touch. DaTscan abnormal bilateral R>L. Levodopa challenge positive at 32%. Autonomic testing normal. PSG confirms RBD (32% RWA). All internally consistent. Lab interpretation mentions HbA1c 6.8% but actual value is 7.4%.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G20 correct. Differential adds NPH and ET as considerations alongside DIP. Critical actions well-targeted. Good emphasis on not diagnosing DIP without DaTscan when premotor features present.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Third metoclopramide case in a row. The NPH differential from MRI adds slight complexity, but the core teaching point is identical to M01 and M02.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 75M African American with DM2, HTN, GERD, gastroparesis, BPH, chronic back pain. Highly realistic geriatric profile.

educational_value.notes: The NPH differential from MRI ventricular enlargement adds modest incremental value over M01/M02. Brother with essential tremor adds a differential consideration. But overall, third repetition of the same core confound significantly dilutes educational value.

issues_found: Third consecutive metoclopramide confound case - significant repetitiveness across M01/M02/M03
Lab interpretation states HbA1c 6.8% but actual value is 7.4%
Chloride 112 mEq/L consistently elevated across multiple PD cases without clinical significance
strengths: NPH differential from MRI adds complexity
Brother with ET provides additional differential consideration
Temporal uncertainty about symptom onset vs drug initiation adds realism
